subject: Re: Warp/Dynamo/Relion Processing of a big dataset
body:
Hi Piotr,

1. For multiple datasets, if you have not already done so, then I'd highly 
recommend re-naming some tilt-series if you foresee naming conflicts (e.g., 
if there is a TS_001 in multiple datasets that will eventually be merged) 
in order to save some later headaches.  

     For reference, I tend to process each data set on its own (usually a 
subset of tilt-series of what look "best" to generate an initial reference 
before working with everything) and merge the different data sets at a 
later step.
     This "later step" for me is typically M. Extracting in M for a 
population with multiple data sources (Warp projects) will extract all 
particles and handle the paths to all particle images in the resultant star 
file (i.e., if 3 data sets are merged, then 1  
     extraction is all that is needed).

     However, the datasets can be merged at any point along the way so long 
as one has a system of keeping track of all the different file paths. When 
extracting in Warp using a star file with particles from different Warp 
projects, the program will only   
     extract particles for that Warp project (i.e., if 3 data sets are 
merged, then 3 separate extractions will be necessary using the same star 
file in order to extract all particles).
     The resultant star files can be readily merged using the 
relion_star_handler.

2. If you can see your particle of interest that is a great sign to move 
forward!
    If you can figure out a way to do a curation step to remove clearly 
poor tilt-series, then it is highly recommended to do so at some point 
before attempting to average over the entire data set.
    For new projects, it is greatly advised to start with a small subset of 
what you can determine are the "best" tilt-series in order to generate a 
reliable initial reference, as well as iterate quickly on what may be 
good      
    parameters for initial alignments that can then be applied to the whole 
data set.

Hope that helps,
-Tom

On Friday, April 29, 2022 at 1:58:08 AM UTC-7 pts...@gmail.com wrote:

> I am just starting with WARP/Dynamo/Relion pipeline and have 2 questions 
> regarding the processing. I have quite a big dataset (hundreds of 
> tomograms) divided into different folders from different data collections. 
> I would be extremely thankful if anyone could make the following points 
> clear to me.
>
> 1. Can I process datasets in separate folders and how will that look later 
> when I will pick particles in dynamo and process them back in WARP and then 
> Relion. Will these programs find all the files in different folders and 
> subfolders or should I keep everything together?
>
> 2. How much should I worry about reconstruction at the very beginning. 
> Since the dataset is large i can't really go through all tilts and not go 
> crazy (WARP is not really helping in deleting bad ones like ice chunks and 
> so on). Sometimes I can see my particles but I can tell that reconstruction 
> could be better. Will another round of reconstruction in sub-volumes deal 
> with it or should I already pay more attention to it before picking 
> particles. So the question is: are visible particles good enough to go 
> further?
>
> Thanks a lot for any input and tips!
>
> Have a great Friday,
> Piotr
>
